sentences.text: I believe I was in the middle of telling that this is, that the Holocaust story is going to be the longest untold story ever told.
And I am quite convinced that that will be the case.
You know, perhaps my own concentration camp career, if you would forgive me for the expression, can be an illustration of one aspect of this inexpressibility of the camp.
I mean you take me, I was fundamentally incarcerated for exactly one year.
In one year, I went to Auschwitz.
I went through France in [INAUDIBLE].

I went from there through the better part of Germany, through Kochendorf, Heilbronn am Neckar, Dachau, and finally [INAUDIBLE], and then was still presumably being transported to a place that was rumored to be [INAUDIBLE] in Austria, where we were all supposed to have been either shot or drowned into a lake, so that no trace of us would be left.
And the things experienced, learned, and the things done or not done, felt and thought, would take my whole life, not the years that I have left, but the years I have lived already, to describe.
And then I would not come nearly to the point where I will have adequately described what I had gone through.
And this has really nothing to do with whether or not I was beaten, or whether I was driven.
It's the life of one year and of six camps is, each moment of it was an eternity.
Finally, as I said before, we were shuttled to the Tutzing and were liberated.
And unlike I suppose 99 and 9, 99% of the survivors, I had an address.
When we were deported, we all of course etched into our heart and into our mind our father's, sister's address where he had lived.
And we all agreed that whoever of us survives, that is going to be the meeting place for us.
And so when I grabbed the first American soldier, and wrote, and told him to advise my father that I'm alive.
I would say that it took no more than maybe a week or 10 days when he turned to me.
And incidentally, in honor of this soldiers, Private [?

Barrant, ?] who my sister and I have rediscovered maybe about two years ago, and who has since died-- it should, I should say that he went about Germany buttonholing survivors and asking them whether they had relatives, or friends, or anybody that they wished to notify through him of their survival.
This was his way of trying to help the survivors.

Anyway, within about 7 to 10 days, he sought me out, I think still in the hospital in Tutzing, and brought back a letter from my father and two of my sisters who wrote me with the, in their own handwriting.
And this was an almost unbelievable occurrence.
When the survivors heard of it, again, the same kind of hope welled up in the minds of a lot of people.
Listen, if Phillip has three sisters already in America, well only, we were only liberated 10 days ago.
It must be that a lot of, if not all, of our women and families have survived.
I was liberated on V-E Day, which officially was May 8.
We were really I think liberated probably after the surrender was signed by Admiral Donitz, a few hours after that.
And then as I started to say, I went to Feldafing and--
Yeah.
Well, I was pretty well run down long before that, along about October or so of 1944, in other words, about eight, nine months into the camp period.
I was so weakened, as many others were before me and after me, that I had begun to give up.
It is, in fact, as the result of that, that I got to Dachau.
I was sent to Dachau what they called in Germany a [INAUDIBLE] for recovery.
Not many people would have, in their right mind, undertaken to volunteer to go.
But I had reached the point, not physically, but emotionally and mentally, where I was at the verge of giving up.
It was, it was a very critical moment for me.
I have described that in detail at, in the [INAUDIBLE] taping, I discovered one day when we were at, most unusually, at relative leisure in a camp, and I said to myself suddenly, you bastard.
Today you didn't think of your mother and sisters.
And that to me spelled the end of my humanity.
And so I began to give up.
Fortunately enough, apparently I still had enough mental reserves that with just a little bit of time in Dachau where I didn't have to work.
And even though that maybe the food rations were smaller, at least I didn't have to work.
And so maybe more of the calories stayed with me.
And I began to recover.
And then I was sent to [INAUDIBLE] got into the kitchen, which enabled me to have a little more food.
And which enabled me also to bring some food out for others, including, most particularly, for our [INAUDIBLE] rabbi who insisted on remaining kosher.
And so I recovered.
And I'm alive and only because of that.
Because I had apparently enough emotional, and psychological, and mental reserves to preserve my humanity.
That hasn't, you know, never ceased to be a preoccupation, a futile one most of the times for myself and several others who were, like myself, were what should I say, aware and conscious people.
At one, on one occasion when we were being taken from [INAUDIBLE] to, from [INAUDIBLE] to, back to Germany we were stopped at the station of Luxembourg.
And we were in open cars, you know, coal cars.
And we, there was an air raid.
And the SS spread out in the fields around the rails.
And a young man and myself, we wanted to make a break for it.
Unfortunately, before we could finally manage to make a break for it, the air raid alarm was called off and suddenly the SS was coming back.
And it was too late.
And again, you have this, this interplay of the time question.

At [INAUDIBLE], I am to this day convinced that the local underground and some of the other different kind of forced laborers who were not Jews, Poles, and Czechs, et cetera, who were in the, in the area were, and particularly the French underground, were about ready to assist us if we made a move.
And again, it was an interplay of the time that just didn't work on the one hand.

And on the other hand, unfortunately, one of the leaders of our camp that we recognized as our leader, translated the warning of the camp commandant that we shouldn't try anything, word for word saying that every second man would be shot out of the line.
And of course, we were all paralyzed.
Who was going to take it upon himself to be responsible for having his fellow prisoners shot out from the line?
So we-- And this was in which camp?
This work in France, in [INAUDIBLE], just as we were being evacuated from there.
And but you know, to some of us the young, so to say, and the strong, and it most required mental and emotional strength, besides just physical strength.
The notion of attempting to, to escape was never too far.
And you know, we talked of it, and we tried to figure out how it could be done.
And by the time we would try to figure, we managed to figure out what we thought might be a good way of doing it, the opportunity disappeared.
Well, we would be generally awakened during the time when it was still dark.
It couldn't have been more than 4:00, 4:30, or something like that.
And we would have probably about 15 minutes or so to prepare ourselves.

Which in the case of some of us, and unfortunately not all of us, meant that we would rush to this trough over which a pipe was dripping water and we'd wash ourselves to the best extent that we could, and of course, go out to you urinate or so.
And then we would stand in line.
And we would get this black fluid that was considered coffee.

And then we would be herded into the five in a row and marched out of the camp under SS guard, with it going through this village of [INAUDIBLE], where on occasion apparently, some Hungarian woman lived there.
I don't know, must have been married or something like that then.
There would be children or women would stand along the street and throw us some food.
Or children would run into the line, and before they were caught, and stick a piece of bread or something into our hands.
And we would go through, incidentally, this is, sounds like a heartwarming kind of story.
The more important aspect of it is that this has happened in my Germany, that is, that we were being marched through the cities and the states-- I mean, and the villages.
And aside from all other facts of life, the German claim that they didn't know is, on the face of it, so false.
I mean as I have once said to, many years ago, to a German in Frankfurt, you didn't see me, but I saw you.
And you can't tell me that you don't know about me.
Because we were marched through the cities, and villages, and towns.
All they, all they had to see, the condition that we were in, and the manner in which we were being treated, even just as we were being driven to work or back to the camp.
That was enough for anyone.
I mean, you know, the brutality with which we were driven in and out of camp was absolutely enough for anybody.
And you know, the butts of the carbines, or whatever the heck they were carrying, and the shouting, and the dogs around us.
And if somebody fell, they would right away start tearing us all apart, you know.
There was no way in which any civilian German, even if he did not live in a, in a town or a city where we were being driven across, but it's enough if you are driven across one town.
There is no way in which word of that wouldn't have spread all through the country.

As I say, this is quite independent of all the other things, you know, like the soldiers writing home, and coming home on furloughs, and telling the stories, and the photographs, and the pictures, and the films, and all sorts of other things.
I'm just taking this one little elementary fact of life.
We were visible.
And the Germans could and did see us.
But they really didn't see us for a completely different reason.
We were, after all, not human beings.
We were by their definition untermensch.
And they were the ubermensch.
And even those who perhaps were not imprisoned in the Nazi ideology, they, it's not only they closed their eyes because it was better not to see and not to know, but they, we really didn't count.
And by the way, this is quite mutual.
I mean to me, a German SS man was also not human.
He was an SS man.
He was a Nazi.
He was a killer.
He was a murderer.
He was not a human being, not someone that I, with the rarest exception, that I would relate even in my mind as to a human being.
It's very important that we realize how this can happen to human being.
That they are dehumanized, not only in fact, but in each other's eyes and consciousness.
That was in France.
In France?
In France, there was, there was a resistance.
And they, and they were oppressed by the Germans and felt oppressed by the Germans.
So certainly there was a kind of commiseration, so to say.
Well, they could see it.
All you had to do was to look at us.
We were walking skeletons, even though that I was in [INAUDIBLE] maybe for about three months or so, so it was three months of after arriving in Auschwitz.
All you had to do was to look at us and you would see what condition we were in.
And again, in that particular case, you know, they were passing by, people were passing by on the road.
Our camp was in a little valley and the road was in a little elevation.
And they could see the camp.
And I could see what was happening.
They recognized that they must have been starving, because of the way they appeared and they looked.
And you know, a lot of people, of course, were also aware of the fact that the Germans themselves didn't have too much.
You know, people will probably never be able to quite recognize what hunger can do to you.
Hunger is not just a desire to eat.
Hunger is something that breaks down your, your being, your identity and your humanness.

When everything else in the world ceases to exist, the only thing that you feel and that you are aware of is that, that terrible pain, that, that urge, that, that unbelievable desire to stuff something into your mouth.
You become, you know, virtually like a single-celled animal that wants to put the food down in one end and let it come out on the other end, and nothing else exists but that one, single function.
Hunger, you can't imagine what it does to human beings.
It's not, it's not just starvation or famine, it destroys your human identity.

You, as I said, you know, nothing exists either in reality, or emotionally, or mentally, except that, that pain, that urge, that, that readiness to or to fall upon anything that you can put into your mouth.
I don't know whether or not in my case, or for that matter, the case of the camps that I was in, is typical at all or in any way, you know, worse than in other places, it was in other places.
You have to remember that we were a relatively valued group.
We were hired out originally to [INAUDIBLE], which was responsible for the manufacturing to the Air Force.
And so we had Air Force, an Air Force group that was supervising us.
And they were, by no means, many of them as bad as the SS.
In the first place, they don't consider themselves guards.
They were guarding the interest of the Air Force and of getting the fuselages done.
The SS would be beyond the perimeter of the camp, usually.
They guarded the camp.
But once we went to work and we were put down into the mine, there were maybe one or two SS men around.
But we were basically working under Air Force and [INAUDIBLE] supervision.
Sure, we, they erected a special tent where they tortured him.
And they would not let him die for a whole week.
And all week long this horrible, inhuman shriek and cry was emanating from that, from that tent.
It was the most horrible experience.
And we with it went our impotence, our inability to do anything for him or about him.
I'm at it again, we tried to figure out how we could somehow manage to get some food to him, or something.
Or even a knife so he could kill himself.
And it just--
This was in Kochendorf, I think.
No.
I recall that he was a tinsmith in our town.
I don't remember his name.
This, by the way, no.
The guards didn't have name.
You know, this brings me back to this business of us not being humans.
I mean, we were at best, a number.
So we even forgot our own names.
Certainly, our, the names of our fellows, you know.
There were some of us, you know, who managed to more or less stay together at one point or another for one time or another.
But it's, there could be no permanence to any kind of relationship.
There couldn't be any real continuity.
Well, the working day more or less was 12 hours, plus the time of going and coming, which probably, if you add it all up, wound up between 15 to 17 hours.
You would get three, four hours sleep at most.
The working conditions in the mines certainly in France weren't so bad, in particular, because there were about a half a million forced laborers of all kinds within the vicinity.
And the sabotage was rampant.
I mean, you know, the lights would go out suddenly in the, in the mine and everything would halt.
And so you know, we were, we were not that terribly badly treated.
I mean, exceptionally from beyond the rules of what was going on in the camps, at times.
It depended on particular times.
Sure.
